attributes.stem:
An eight-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with lifelong dry, scaly skin. His parent and sibling have similar symptoms. He has a history of atopic dermatitis and asthma but takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical, fine white scales particularly prominent on his legs and trunk, areas of lichenification, and with relative sparing of the antecubital and popliteal fossae. The palms show increased skin markings.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of ichthyosis vulgaris, an autosomal semi-dominant condition characterised by generalised dry, scaly skin with flexural sparing. It presents with xerosis (dry skin) without much inflammation which can appear like 'fish scales', lichenification, fissuring and palmar hyperlinearity. The condition is often associated with atopic disorders and typically presents before age 5 years (not typically at birth). The mutation in FLG causes complete or partial deficiency of filaggrin, and occurs in about 8 -10% of the general population with variations in severity.

Regular emollients are the cornerstone of management for ichthyosis vulgaris. They should be applied liberally and frequently, particularly after bathing while the skin is still damp. This helps to maintain skin hydration and prevent scale build-up. Emollients with keratolytic properties (containing urea or salicylic acid) can be particularly helpful. The condition is life-long though improves with age.

Topical corticosteroids are not indicated for routine management of ichthyosis vulgaris as they do not address the underlying disorder of cornification. They may be useful for managing acute flares of associated eczema but should not be used long-term due to side effects.

Oral retinoids such as acitretin are reserved for severe cases of ichthyosis, particularly non-vulgaris variants that are more severe. They carry significant side effects and require careful monitoring, making them inappropriate as first-line therapy for typical ichthyosis vulgaris.

Topical calcineurin inhibitors like tacrolimus have no role in the routine management of ichthyosis vulgaris. They may be useful for associated atopic dermatitis but do not address the underlying scaling.

Phototherapy is not typically used for ichthyosis vulgaris. While it might provide some benefit in reducing scaling, it is not practical or necessary as first-line therapy when simpler measures like emollients are usually effective.
learningPoints: Ichthyosis vulgaris is characterised by dry, scaly skin with sparing of flexural areas.
Regular emollients are the cornerstone of ichthyosis management.
references: Lee N. Ichthyosis vulgaris. DermNet NZ. 2014.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/ichthyosis-vulgaris
Jaffar H, Shakir Z, Kumar G, Ali IF. Ichthyosis vulgaris: An updated review. Skin Health Disease. 2022.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9892433/
